The WSO spent around 36 hours on the run behind enemy lines.

Extended survival and recovery operations behind hostile lines demonstrate critical importance of combat search-and-rescue capabilities and aircrew survival training protocols.

Aerospace Apr 5, 2026

Thinking of You, Earth

On April 4, 2026, NASA astronaut and Artemis II Commander Reid Wiseman peers out of one of the Orion spacecraft’s main cabin windows, looking back at Earth, as the crew travels towards the Moon. The Artemis II astronauts – Wiseman and fellow NASA astronauts Christina Koch and Victor Glover, and CSA...

Artemis II's lunar trajectory will provide unprecedented high-resolution Earth imagery from deep space, offering both scientific value and public engagement for sustained space exploration funding.

A Look At The Salaries Of Air Traffic Controllers In 2026

These controllers are working overtime.

Rising overtime demand signals staffing shortages that may strain ATC capacity and delay modernization initiatives across the National Airspace System.

Safety Apr 5, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation from accident investigation to predictive risk identification, reducing incidents before they occur rather than studying them afterward.

Commercial Apr 5, 2026

Never Made: The Non-Existent Widebody Aircraft That Hawaiian Airlines Ordered

These jets never actually entered production.

Hawaiian Airlines' canceled widebody orders reveal how airlines sometimes commit to aircraft that never materialize due to manufacturer delays or strategic pivots.

Military Apr 5, 2026

Why Does The US Air Force Still Rely On The B-52?

The B-52 stays relevant thanks to its low operating costs, its plentiful remaining flight hours, and the ability to use stand-off munitions.

The B-52's cost-effectiveness and compatibility with modern stand-off weapons extend its operational viability beyond traditional airframe lifespan expectations.

Commercial Apr 5, 2026

IndiGo Has A New CEO: Former British Airways And IAG Chief Willie Walsh

There’s a huge leadership shakeup at the top of India’s largest airline, IndiGo. The airline recently saw the departure of Pieter Elbers who had been leading the carrier since 2022 after leaving his role as the head of KLM. In his place will be Willie Walsh - who will presumably be leaving his role...

Walsh's appointment signals IndiGo's strategy shift toward leveraging international airline operational expertise as it navigates competitive pressures and fleet expansion in Asia's fastest-growing market.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 5, 2026

The Airlines That Could Benefit From Boeing’s All-New 787 Dreamliners

How the upgraded widebody will improve flying around the world.

Airlines operating long-haul routes to underserved markets can now access premium widebody economics previously requiring larger fleets or higher seating densities.

The 30-minute turnaround requires coordinated execution across multiple simultaneous tasks—cleaning, restocking, safety checks, and repairs—that directly impact airline schedule reliability and operational costs.

Military Apr 5, 2026

[Updated] U.S. Rescues Downed F-15E WSO Deep Inside Iran

The mission to rescue the isolated airman inside Iran has been described as one of the most challenging and complex in the history of U.S. special operations. The Weapons Systems Officer (WSO) who ejected from the downed F-15E Strike Eagle belonging to the 48th Fighter Wing of the U.S. Air Force in...

Complex combat rescue operations require unprecedented coordination between fighter, special operations, and support assets to extract personnel from hostile territory.

Aerospace Apr 5, 2026

NASA finalizes science plans for Artemis 2 lunar flyby

As Artemis 2 approaches the moon, a NASA science team is finalizing plans for the observations they want the astronauts to perform during their flyby.

Artemis 2's science objectives will depend on real-time crew observations rather than pre-programmed instruments, requiring astronauts to make critical data-collection decisions during the lunar flyby.

Commercial Apr 5, 2026

Stopping Power: Boeing 777-9 Brakes Reach 2,500 Degrees In Critical Aborted Takeoff Test

The aircraft pushes the limits of its advanced braking system during a simulated maximum-weight, high-speed aborted takeoff.

Boeing's advanced braking system withstands extreme thermal stress, validating safety margins for the aircraft's maximum certified takeoff weight and speed envelope.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 5, 2026

Press Release: TAP’s new longhaul cabin, Economy Prime, available June 1

TAP Air Portugal announces the launch of Economy Prime, a new long‑haul cabin designed to offer customers more space, additional privacy and a superior travel experience within the Economy segment. Flights featuring the new Economy Prime cabin start June 1, 2026, and this cabin is now available for...

TAP's Economy Prime segmentation strategy allows carriers to monetize premium economy positioning without cannibilizing full-cabin reconfiguration investments.

Frederick Municipal is a small general and corporate aviation airport located just east of the city of Frederick, Maryland. The traffic mix runs the spectrum from single engine training aircraft up...

Frederick Municipal Airport demonstrates how regional GA facilities serve dual roles supporting both flight training pipelines and corporate aviation demand in mid-Atlantic markets.

Military Apr 4, 2026

U.S. F-16 Shown with Mine-Dispensing Cluster Bombs

CENTCOM has released photos of F-16s loaded with cluster bombs for operations in Iran, a week after BLU-91/B ‘Gator’ anti-tank mines were found on the ground. The U.S. Central Command has released on Apr. 4, 2026, new photos of U.S. Air Force F-16CM Fighting Falcon fighter jets (specifically Block...

Cluster munition deployment by F-16s represents an escalation in kinetic operations within the region, signaling intensified tactical readiness against potential Iranian targets.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Atlas 5 launches Amazon Leo satellites

An Atlas 5 launched the latest set of satellites for Amazon’s broadband constellation April 4 as the company seeks to accelerate deployment of its spacecraft.

Amazon's accelerated Leo deployment signals intensifying competition in satellite broadband, creating new payload opportunities for established launch providers like ULA.

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

NASA Answers Your Most Pressing Artemis II Questions

The first crewed test flight under NASA’s Artemis program is underway. Four Artemis II astronauts are flying aboard NASA’s Orion spacecraft around the Moon and back, as they test how the spacecraft’s systems operate in a deep space environment. NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and...

Artemis II's operational testing of Orion systems in lunar orbit directly informs spacecraft reliability requirements for future crewed deep-space missions and commercial lunar ventures.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

SES eyes cross-unit content bundles between media and aero

Multi-orbit satellite operator SES is exploring cross-unit content bundles between its media and aero or cruise businesses, as the passenger experience evolves. The revelation, made by SES senior product executive Blane Boynton during the SATShow conference and exhibition in Washington D.C., comes...

SES is integrating satellite media capabilities with aviation connectivity to create bundled passenger experience offerings across airlines and cruise operators.

See Runway Girl Network →

Industry Apr 5, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-flight systems offers near-term emissions reductions without waiting for new aircraft technologies, directly addressing capacity constraints while sustainability is still achieved.

See MDPI →

Military Apr 4, 2026

Mission to Recover Downed F-15E Aircrew Puts Combat Search And Rescue in the Spotlight

The loss of an F-15E Strike Eagle in Iran prompted specialized Combat Search and Rescue units to spring into action, with a multitude of assets working together to recover all missing aircrew. Here’s how. On Apr. 3, 2026, multiple specialized units sprang into action for a complex mission they...

Combat Search and Rescue operations require synchronized coordination across multiple specialized military units, demonstrating the critical infrastructure investments needed to support downed aircrew recovery in contested environments.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Space Force budget would more than double in Trump’s $1.5 trillion defense plan

$71 billion request for the U.S. Space Force for fiscal year 2027 includes more than $60 billion for procurement, research and development...

Space Force's $71B budget prioritizes procurement and R&D, signaling accelerated development of space-based capabilities that will reshape military aviation operations and satellite-dependent systems.

Military Apr 4, 2026

Iran’s RQ-170 Clone Destroyed in U.S. Strikes, CENTCOM Video Shows

A video shared by CENTCOM shows the destruction of an Iranian clone of the RQ-170 Sentinel, which was reverse-engineered after crashing in Iran 15 years ago. An Iranian clone of the secretive U.S. RQ-170 Sentinel ISR drone was apparently destroyed in an airstrike, an Apr. 2, 2026 video by the U.S....

Iran's reverse-engineered RQ-170 clone demonstrates both the vulnerability of advanced drone technology to capture and the ongoing operational risks of deploying copied systems in contested airspace.

See The Aviationist →

Illuminated in Orion

NASA astronaut Christina Koch reads on a tablet in the dimly lit Orion crew capsule in this April 3, 2026, photo. To the right of the image’s center, CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen is seen in profile peering out of one of Orion’s windows. Lights are turned off to avoid glare on...

Orion's interior lighting design prioritizes window visibility for lunar observation while maintaining crew comfort during extended missions, a critical human factors consideration for deep space exploration vehicles.

Trump budget seeks $372 million cut to US Essential Air Service program

US President Donald Trump’s fiscal 2027 budget proposal takes aim at the Essential Air Service program, calling for…...

Cutting Essential Air Service subsidies would force rural communities to rely entirely on ground transportation, potentially reshaping regional aviation network economics and carrier route planning.

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

NASA Sets Coverage for Northrop Grumman’s CRS-24 Resupply Launch

NASA, Northrop Grumman, and SpaceX are targeting no earlier than 8:49 a.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 8, for the next launch delivering science investigations, supplies, and equipment to the International Space Station. Filled with approximately 11,000 pounds of cargo, the Northrop Grumman Cygnus XL...

Northrop Grumman's upgraded Cygnus XL variant increases ISS resupply capacity by leveraging SpaceX launch infrastructure, expanding commercial cargo delivery options for space operations.

Commercial Apr 3, 2026

Press Release: SUM Air orders up to eight ATR 72-600s

ATR, the world’s leading regional aircraft manufacturer, today announced that SUM Air, Korea’s newest regional airline, has placed an order for four brand‑new ATR 72‑600 aircraft, along with four additional purchase rights, with deliveries scheduled from 2028. The agreement was signed on the...

SUM Air's commitment to ATR 72-600s signals confidence in regional turboprop economics for Korea's emerging carrier market despite competitive pressures from larger operators.

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

Michael Graham Named NTSB Vice Chairman

The certified transport pilot and NTSB board member will co-head the agency for a three-year term.

Graham's operational flying background as a certified transport pilot brings direct accident investigation experience to the NTSB's leadership during a period of increased aviation safety scrutiny.
